info.title: getting started
info.text: This demo is a companion to the MusicVAE demo. Both generate a short melody and play it back with a highlighted score display. The difference is that MusicVAE uses a machine learning model to do it, while here we write the musical logic ourselves. The goal is to show that sometimes the handwritten approach is not only simpler, it can also be more expressive and intentional.

info.title: libraries
info.text: We only need two libraries: the nn library and Tone.js. Compare this to MusicVAE which required four imports including TensorFlow.js and a Magenta module. Writing the logic ourselves means less to download and no dependency on external model weights.

info.title: global vars
info.text: We only need two global variables: synth for the Tone.js polyphonic synthesizer, and score for the div that displays the notes.

info.title: setup
info.text: The setup function runs on page load. We create a Tone.PolySynth routed to the audio output, a button wired to playMelody, and a score display div. This is essentially the same setup as MusicVAE, the playback infrastructure is identical.

info.title: root and scale
info.text: The heart of this demo is the generateMelody function. We start by picking a random root note from a set of notes in the fourth octave, then a random mode using nn.randomMode(). A mode is a pattern of intervals (like major, minor, dorian, etc.) that gives a scale its character. nn.createScale(root, mode) builds the full ordered array of note names for that combination, this is the list of notes our melody will draw from.

info.title: timing and starting position
info.text: noteCount is a random integer between 4 and 16, so each melody has a different length. stepDur is 0.25 seconds per step (quarter notes at 120 BPM), and now sets a small lookahead offset so Tone.js has time to schedule reliably. degree is our current position in the scale, we start at a random index and will move from there.

info.title: random walk
info.text: For each note in the melody we pick a move from the array [-2, -1, -1, 0, 1, 1, 2] and add it to degree. This is a random walk, the melody steps up or down the scale by one or two degrees at a time, with some chance of repeating a note (value 0). The weighted array makes steps of one more common than leaps of two, which tends to sound more melodic. The clamping ensures degree never goes out of bounds.

info.title: note and timing per step
info.text: We look up the actual note name at the current degree in the scale, pick a random duration from a small set of short values, and compute the absolute start time by adding the step index multiplied by stepDur. We push both the note and its timing into arrays, and call updateScore to add it to the visual display as we go.

info.title: updateScore and highlightNote
info.text: These two helper functions are identical to the ones in the MusicVAE demo. updateScore creates a span for each note with a unique id and adds it to the score div. highlightNote resets all spans to grey then colors the active one blue. Reusing the same display logic across both demos makes the comparison cleaner, any difference in output comes purely from how the melody was generated.

info.title: scheduling playback
info.text: The playMelody function is also structurally identical to MusicVAE's: we loop over the notes, schedule each one with synth.triggerAttackRelease(), and queue the visual highlight with setTimeout at the matching delay. The only difference is our timing data came from simple arithmetic rather than a model's quantized step format.

info.title: algorithmic vs ML
info.text: Both demos produce a melody and play it back the same way, but the source of that melody is completely different. The ML approach (MusicVAE) learned patterns from a large dataset and samples from a latent space, it produces melodies that "sound human" but you have limited control over why they sound that way. The algorithmic approach gives you full authorship: you decide the scale, the rhythm, the motion rules. For many composers this directness is the art. Neither is better, the right choice depends on what you're trying to make.
